name: Amy Beliveau
background: Six-year Winchester resident, Town Meeting member for precinct seven, with experience in real estate, development, design and negotiation. She also cited service on Town Meeting, the EFPBC, and the State of the Town Committee.
summary: Amy Beliveau won a Planning Board seat in 2026 as a challenger who emphasized balanced growth, housing production, and practical real-estate and development experience.
locationNote: Winchester resident; Town Meeting member representing precinct seven.
priorRecordLabel: Town Meeting and local committee service
priorRecordSummary: Beliveau presented her Town Meeting service and work on the EFPBC and State of the Town Committee as relevant preparation, alongside professional experience in real estate and development.

stanceCards.label: No clear school facilities position in provided sources
stanceCards.detail: The provided Planning Board sources do not show a specific stance from Beliveau on school facilities.

stanceCards.label: Supports expanding housing options
stanceCards.detail: At the Planning Board forum, Beliveau argued Winchester must add housing and suggested looking beyond transit-oriented corridors to interior conversions of large homes in single-family neighborhoods.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes financially informed planning decisions
stanceCards.detail: In her candidate profile, Beliveau said she understands project financials and what levers the town can use to create value and meet objectives.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines development and town-government experience
evidence.summary: Beliveau said she would bring more than a decade of development, design and negotiation experience, plus service on Town Meeting, the EFPBC, and the State of the Town Committee. She described herself as a data-driven decision maker focused on balanced, thoughtful growth.

evidence.title: Forum highlights housing and preservation approach
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Beliveau said she brings real estate industry knowledge the board lacks and argued that historic value and larger-scale housing opportunities are not mutually exclusive.

evidence.title: Beliveau backs broader housing strategies
evidence.summary: Beliveau urged Winchester to look beyond MBTA Communities transit-oriented corridors and consider interior conversions of large homes in single-family neighborhoods.

evidence.title: Official 2026 election results
evidence.summary: In the two-seat Planning Board race, Beliveau received 2,042 votes, finishing second behind Keri Layton and ahead of Nicholas Rossettos, winning a seat.

evidence.title: Election-night report says Beliveau unseated incumbent
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that newcomer Amy Beliveau unseated incumbent Nicholas Rossettos to claim one of two open Planning Board seats.

name: Heather von Mering
background: Mother of five in Winchester Public Schools; works as a vice president in construction and asset planning. She has served for 16 years in Winchester town government, including the Historical Commission, Planning Board, Capital Planning Committee, Town Meeting, and as town moderator.
summary: Von Mering ran for School Committee in 2026 emphasizing parent experience, literacy reform, special education advocacy, facilities planning, and municipal finance experience. She argued the district should focus on closing literacy and math gaps and strengthening supports before taking on new commitments. She lost the race to John Bellaire.
locationNote: Winchester resident; moved to Winchester in 2009.
priorRecordSummary: Her public record is primarily through Winchester town government service and school-related advocacy rather than prior School Committee service. Sources describe work on literacy reform and special education advocacy, plus a 2025 Town Meeting amendment supporting the school budget as requested.

stanceCards.label: Pushes literacy reform
stanceCards.detail: She described herself as a long-time advocate for literacy reform, especially for students with dyslexia and language-based learning disabilities, and supported the district’s new literacy curriculum while cautioning that early data would be unreliable.

stanceCards.label: Supported funding the requested school budget
stanceCards.detail: At 2025 Town Meeting, she said Town Meeting should fund the school budget as requested and opposed using Circuit Breaker funds for the FY26 budget.

stanceCards.label: Facilities and capital planning background
stanceCards.detail: She cited her construction and asset-planning work as informing her approach to facilities governance, capital planning, budgeting, and stewardship of school buildings.

stanceCards.label: Wants expert-led technology policy
stanceCards.detail: On AI and school technology policy, she said the School Committee should rely on outside expertise and that she was not the expert to draft that policy alone.

stanceCards.label: Called for more attention to student mental health
stanceCards.detail: Election-night coverage said both candidates called for greater attention to student mental health.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines parent, advocate, professional, and town leader experience
evidence.summary: Von Mering said she would bring perspectives as a parent of five in Winchester schools, a literacy and special education advocate, a construction and asset-planning professional, and a longtime town leader with municipal finance experience.

evidence.title: Forum coverage highlights literacy advocacy and early intervention views
evidence.summary: At the candidate forum, she said she had advocated for curriculum changes since at least 2017, described organizing families around dyslexia identification, and argued early support lowers later costs.

evidence.title: Forum comments on technology policy
evidence.summary: Discussing AI in schools, she said the School Committee should rely on outside expertise and that she was not an expert suited to write technology policy alone.

evidence.title: Town Meeting amendment supporting requested school budget
evidence.summary: In 2025, von Mering moved to amend the school budget to the School Committee recommendation with additional free cash, saying Town Meeting should fund the school budget as requested and not use Circuit Breaker funds for FY26.

evidence.title: Election-night summary of campaign message
evidence.summary: Election-night coverage said von Mering argued the district should close literacy and math gaps before taking on new commitments, and that both candidates called for greater attention to student mental health and said Winchester lacked a coherent technology policy.

evidence.title: 2026 School Committee election result
evidence.summary: Official results show John H. Bellaire received 2,192 votes to Heather R. von Mering’s 1,861 in the 2026 School Committee race.

name: John Bellaire
background: Winchester High School graduate and Brown University graduate who studied education and public policy. He works full time for Bellwether, a national education nonprofit focused on state school finance, and has described himself as an education researcher, youth mentor, and Winchester Public Schools graduate.
summary: Bellaire presented himself as a school-finance-oriented education policy researcher with local ties to Winchester Public Schools. In campaign materials and forum coverage, he emphasized equitable and transparent school finance, earlier literacy intervention, student mental health, student opportunities, and attention to technology in the classroom.
locationNote: Winchester Public Schools graduate; worked as a substitute teacher at Vinson-Owen Elementary during the 2021-2022 school year and as Weekend Mentor for Winchester A Better Chance.
priorRecordLabel: Education policy and local civic experience
priorRecordSummary: Bellaire's cited experience is mainly in education research, school finance modeling, school quality reviews, youth mentoring, substitute teaching, and local Town Meeting service rather than prior School Committee service.

stanceCards.label: Budget and finance focus
stanceCards.detail: He said his work includes developing financial models to help build equitable and transparent school finance systems and described experience interrogating school budgets.

stanceCards.label: Push for earlier literacy action
stanceCards.detail: At the forum, he said Winchester had been delayed on literacy and argued for earlier support so students do not fall behind and later need more intensive services.

stanceCards.label: Support student mental health
stanceCards.detail: In his candidate profile, he said his experiences as a Winchester student motivate him to support student mental health.

stanceCards.label: Attention to classroom technology
stanceCards.detail: In his candidate profile, he said his experiences motivate him to address technology in the classroom.

evidence.title: Candidate profile highlights education research and finance background
evidence.summary: Bellaire said he works at a leading education nonprofit conducting research and financial modeling to help build equitable and transparent school finance systems, and that these experiences equip him to serve on the School Committee.

evidence.title: Candidate profile cites mentoring, substitute teaching, and Winchester schools background
evidence.summary: He described serving as Weekend Mentor for Winchester A Better Chance, leading youth programming, working as a substitute teacher at Vinson-Owen Elementary in 2021-2022, and graduating from Muraco, McCall, and Winchester High.

evidence.title: Candidate profile names student engagement, technology, and mental health as motivations
evidence.summary: Bellaire said his firsthand student experience in Winchester motivates him to provide high-quality student engagement opportunities, address technology in the classroom, and support student mental health.

evidence.title: Forum coverage describes literacy and special education comments
evidence.summary: At the candidate forum, Bellaire said Winchester had been behind on literacy, cited worsening MCAS reading performance since 2019, and argued that addressing problems in first grade is preferable to waiting until students fall further behind.

evidence.title: Forum coverage summarizes professional background and student-centered framing
evidence.summary: Forum coverage said Bellaire graduated from Winchester High in 2021, studied education and public policy at Brown, works for Bellwether on state school finance, and said every student should have the opportunity to develop into their best selves.

evidence.title: 2026 election result: Bellaire won School Committee seat
evidence.summary: Official town election results show John H. Bellaire received 2,192 votes for School Committee, defeating Heather R. Von Mering's 1,861.

name: Keri Layton
background: Incumbent Planning Board member seeking a second term in 2026. Previously served as a Precinct 2 Town Meeting member, on the Master Plan Steering Committee, and for three years on the Conservation Commission.
summary: Layton presented herself as an experienced incumbent focused on housing production, zoning reform, and balancing development with conservation and town character. She highlighted work on MBTA Communities zoning, the Main Street Mixed Use District, and ADU zoning changes, and won one of two Planning Board seats in 2026.
locationNote: Winchester; previously represented Precinct 2 in Town Meeting and later moved into a historic home in town.
priorRecordLabel: Planning Board incumbent; prior town boards and committees
priorRecordSummary: Layton said she began as a Precinct 2 Town Meeting member in 2017, served on the Master Plan Steering Committee, then on the Conservation Commission for three years. On the Planning Board, she said she chaired the board during MBTA Communities implementation and North Main Street rezoning work, and helped advance the Main Street Mixed Use District and ADU by-right zoning changes.

stanceCards.label: No clear school facilities platform in sources
stanceCards.detail: Available sources focus on planning, zoning, housing growth, and preservation rather than school facilities.

stanceCards.label: Supports added housing supply and affordable housing planning
stanceCards.detail: Layton argued Winchester still needs hundreds of subsidized housing units, supported multifamily zoning near downtown, and said mixed-use projects can expand the commercial tax base.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes planning standards and mixed-use tax base
stanceCards.detail: At the forum, Layton said pre-application meetings should clarify affordable housing requirements, aesthetics, and height expectations, and said mixed-use projects can grow commercial taxes without adding students to schools.

evidence.title: Won reelection to Planning Board in 2026
evidence.summary: Official results show Layton received 2,331 votes, the highest total in the two-seat race, and election-night coverage reported she led the field and won reelection.

evidence.title: Layton describes prior town service and Planning Board work
evidence.summary: Layton said she served as a Town Meeting member, Master Plan Steering Committee representative, and Conservation Commission member before serving on the Planning Board, where she chaired work tied to MBTA Communities zoning, the Main Street Mixed Use District, and ADU by-right legislation.

evidence.title: Forum comments on housing growth and affordable housing needs
evidence.summary: At the candidate forum, Layton said Winchester remained roughly 400 units short of the state subsidized housing threshold and urged creative approaches to distributing housing across town.

evidence.title: Forum comments on development expectations and tax base
evidence.summary: Layton said the board should set expectations early for developers on affordable housing, aesthetics, and height, and said mixed-use projects can expand the commercial tax base without adding students to schools.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage of Planning Board race
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported Layton led the Planning Board field while Amy Beliveau won the second seat over Nicholas Rossettos.

name: Michelle Prior
background: Incumbent Select Board member seeking a second term in 2026. Prior’s profile cites 15 years on Town Meeting representing Precinct 4, six years on the Finance Committee including as FY24 chair, three years on the Select Board including two years as chair, and service on the Audit Advisory Committee, Retiree Health Insurance Advisory Committee, and Wright-Locke Farm Conservancy board.
summary: Prior ran for reelection as an experienced incumbent emphasizing long-term financial planning, added non-property-tax revenue, housing options for different ages and incomes, and cross-board coordination. In override discussions, she argued against asking voters for more than identified needs and after the override loss called for listening sessions and surveys before pursuing another ballot question.
priorRecordLabel: Select Board chair and Finance Committee veteran
priorRecordSummary: Prior was serving on the Select Board during the 2025-26 budget and override debates, chaired the board, and described prior service on Town Meeting and the Finance Committee.

stanceCards.label: Pursues non-property-tax revenue
stanceCards.detail: She called for more revenue through commercial growth, fee reviews, outside-hauler transfer station access, and leases on town assets such as roof space for solar providers.

stanceCards.label: Supports housing options
stanceCards.detail: She said the Select Board should support housing development serving different family configurations and seniors who want to age in place.

stanceCards.label: Favored a narrower override ask
stanceCards.detail: During the override debate, she said the board should not ask voters for money beyond what modeling showed was needed through FY2029.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes planning and coordination
stanceCards.detail: She campaigned on building a long-term financial plan, supporting the new town manager, and improving communication across boards and committees.

stanceCards.label: Linked override funds to facilities needs
stanceCards.detail: The override package she helped negotiate included capital funding for Town Hall restoration and school facilities, though her profile focused more on overall fiscal planning than specific school facility proposals.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines experience and priorities
evidence.summary: Prior described 15 years on Town Meeting, six years on the Finance Committee, and three years on the Select Board with two as chair. She highlighted non-property-tax revenue, housing options, communication across boards, and a long-term financial plan.

evidence.title: State of the Town minutes list Prior as Select Board chair
evidence.summary: Official minutes for the joint State of the Town meeting show Michelle Prior serving as Select Board chair in May 2025.

evidence.title: Prior argued against a larger override cushion
evidence.summary: In Select Board negotiations, Prior said the board should not ask voters for more money than identified needs required and objected to limiting flexibility by tying funds to the health care stabilization account.

evidence.title: After override defeat, Prior urged listening before another ballot question
evidence.summary: Following the override loss, Prior said the town should use focus groups, listening sessions, and surveys to understand why the measure failed before moving ahead, while expecting a new ballot question within the year.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage describes campaign emphasis
evidence.summary: Election-night coverage said Prior emphasized deliberate process and consensus-building and noted her prior six years on the Finance Committee.

evidence.title: 2026 election result
evidence.summary: Official results show Michelle I. Prior defeating Shamus M. Brady 2,849 to 1,159 in the Select Board race.

name: Nick Rossettos
background: Incumbent Planning Board member, Winchester native, and Winchester High School graduate who works as a fractional/consulting CFO for life sciences companies. He said he has spent the past four years working on mixed-use development, MBTA 3A compliance zoning, North Main Street zoning, and design standards in Winchester.
summary: Rossettos ran for reelection to the Planning Board in 2026 emphasizing experienced review of growth, mixed-use and inclusionary housing, and preserving Winchester’s historic and downtown character through design standards. He finished behind two candidates in a two-seat race.
priorRecordSummary: Rossettos described four years of Planning Board work, including unanimous approval of the Waterfield affordable housing mixed-use development, work on MBTA 3A and North Main Street zoning, and design standards. At the 2026 forum he was identified as an incumbent and liaison to the Design Review Committee and Historical Commission.
keyThemes: Commercial tax base alongside residential development

stanceCards.label: No clear schools facilities platform in sources
stanceCards.detail: The available sources do not show a specific position on school buildings or facilities.

stanceCards.label: Supports growth that expands tax base
stanceCards.detail: He said zoning should encourage mixed-use development to increase the town's tax base and argued the town needs a mix of development types.

stanceCards.label: Backs inclusionary and affordable housing
stanceCards.detail: He highlighted approval of affordable housing at Waterfield, support for inclusionary housing in zoning, and attention to 40B design.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes financial discipline and balanced fiscal impact
stanceCards.detail: He cited his CFO background and argued the town needs a combination of residential and other development, not only housing.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on Planning Board experience and priorities
evidence.summary: Rossettos said that over the past four years he worked on mixed-use developments, voted with the board to approve the Waterfield affordable housing mixed-use project, worked on MBTA 3A and North Main Street zoning, and focused on design standards, inclusionary housing, density, and increasing the town's tax base.

evidence.title: Forum remarks on growth and preservation
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Rossettos said growth and preservation are not mutually exclusive and centered his campaign on preserving downtown character through experience, judgment, and sustained effort.

evidence.title: Forum discussion of housing production and 40B design
evidence.summary: Rossettos cited Waterfield, a completed Cambridge Street 40B, the Washington and Swanton 40B proposal, and inclusionary units at 10 Converse Place, saying the town wanted to set an example of what beautiful 40B development can look like. He also suggested creative multi-unit housing ideas that resemble single-family historic homes.

evidence.title: Forum remarks on zoning complexity and fiscal impacts
evidence.summary: Rossettos said some zoning regulations are complicated and confusing. Drawing on his CFO work, he said residential projects are profitable for developers and that the town needs a combination of development types.

evidence.title: 2026 Planning Board election result
evidence.summary: In the two-seat Planning Board race, Rossettos received 1,864 votes, behind Keri Layton (2,331) and Amy S. Beliveau (2,042), so he was not elected.

name: Shamus Brady
background: Public-school administrator and educator; current director of special education for Everett Public Schools. Served on the Winchester School Committee from 2021 to 2024 and previously on the Finance Committee during the early months of the COVID-19 pandemic. Also cites volunteer work with Winchester ABC, youth soccer, and the FAN Board.
summary: Brady ran for Select Board in 2026 emphasizing school-budget experience, support for an operating override, affordability relief, and business recruitment for new revenue. He presented himself as a progressive voice focused on long-term investment and public engagement on budget choices. He lost the 2026 Select Board race.
locationNote: Winchester resident since 2014; father of two children in Winchester public schools.
priorRecordLabel: School Committee and Finance Committee record
priorRecordSummary: Brady’s profile cites service on the Winchester School Committee from 2021-2024 and on the Finance Committee during the pandemic. Source coverage also documents his participation in 2024 School Committee budget and literacy discussions.

stanceCards.label: Supports operating override
stanceCards.detail: Brady said Winchester needed to pass an operating override to maintain current service levels and said he would steward funds according to stated priorities.

stanceCards.label: Calls for affordability measures
stanceCards.detail: He proposed a committee on affordability and pointed to tools such as a residential tax exemption to reduce burdens on vulnerable residents.

stanceCards.label: Seeks new revenue sources
stanceCards.detail: He said the town should recruit businesses and identify revenue-generating uses that fit Winchester, rather than rely only on residential property taxes.

stanceCards.label: Strong school-budget focus
stanceCards.detail: He highlighted long experience with school and district budgets and argued that understanding school finance is important because it is the largest part of the town budget.

stanceCards.label: Would use public process on cuts
stanceCards.detail: If an override failed, he said he would ask department heads to identify essential services and potential cuts, then bring proposals to the public through feedback and forums.

evidence.title: Lost 2026 Select Board race
evidence.summary: Official results show Michelle Prior defeated Shamus M. Brady, 2,849 to 1,159, in the 2026 Select Board election.

evidence.title: Campaign profile: override, affordability, and revenue
evidence.summary: In his candidate profile, Brady said the town needed an operating override, proposed an affordability committee, and called for recruiting businesses to expand revenue without raising residential property taxes.

evidence.title: Campaign profile: school and finance experience
evidence.summary: Brady said he had worked on school budgets since 2008, served on the Winchester School Committee from 2021-2024, and served on the Finance Committee during the early COVID-19 period.

evidence.title: Supported school budget additions in 2024
evidence.summary: At a 2024 School Committee meeting, Brady supported budget proposals that would serve students directly and said the committee should preserve room for literacy-related funding work.

evidence.title: Asked about literacy curriculum access
evidence.summary: At a 2024 School Committee meeting, Brady asked whether the UFLI literacy resource was available to all K-2 teachers in all schools.

evidence.title: Election-night characterization of campaign
evidence.summary: Local election-night coverage described Brady as a former School Committee member and current director of special education who campaigned on urgency, visible dissent, and new revenue streams.

name: Anthea Brady
background: Select Board incumbent seeking re-election in 2025. Brady said she has lived in Winchester for 11 years, holds a Master of Public Policy, previously worked in the education sector with federal, state and local agencies, especially in special education and K-12 data, and currently works with large organizations on culture and compliance.
summary: Brady was re-elected to the Select Board in 2025. In her campaign materials and forum remarks, she emphasized the town’s structural deficit, the likely need for a carefully considered override, continuity in capital planning, support for schools, and continued work on affordable housing, walkability and economic development.
priorRecordSummary: Brady said she had served three years on the Select Board, including two as vice-chair, and previously served on the Personnel Board. Official May 2025 meeting minutes list her as Select Board vice-chair.
keyThemes: Town budget pressure and structural deficit
Possible override alongside short-term use of free cash
Affordable housing at large and small scales, including 160 Forest Street development discussion

stanceCards.label: Supports school investment
stanceCards.detail: Said the school system is critical infrastructure and should be supported with high-quality teachers, curriculum, professional development and classroom supports; later spoke in support of the amended school budget.

stanceCards.label: Open to an override
stanceCards.detail: Said free cash could help in the short run but an override might be necessary; in her profile she said the town was looking at free cash or an operating override and neither was ideal.

stanceCards.label: Backs capital and safety projects
stanceCards.detail: Highlighted support for playground safety work and argued for continuity around a capital plan, while noting Muraco school rebuilding or refurbishment as part of the town’s obligations discussed in the campaign forum.

stanceCards.label: Supports affordable housing growth
stanceCards.detail: Called for advancing both large- and small-scale affordable housing and said neighborhood engagement would matter for 160 Forest Street.

stanceCards.label: Wants economic development to expand tax base
stanceCards.detail: Pointed to mixed-use opportunities near North Main Street, MBTA station reopening and Holton Street as ways to grow commercial activity.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Brady said she had lived in Winchester 11 years, held a Master of Public Policy, worked in education and compliance-related roles, was seeking re-election after three years on the Select Board, and supported school investment, affordable housing and economic development while weighing free cash and a possible override.

evidence.title: Select Board forum comments on budget and capital planning
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Brady said the budget year was critical because costs had risen, free cash could help in the short run, an override might be necessary, and the town needed continuity around a capital plan.

evidence.title: 2025 election results
evidence.summary: Official results show Brady won 1,649 votes in the Select Board race, finishing second behind Paras Bhayani and securing one of two seats.

evidence.title: Winchester News final election summary
evidence.summary: Local election coverage reported that Brady and Paras Bhayani were the winners in the 2025 Select Board race.

evidence.title: May 2025 State of the Town minutes list Brady as vice-chair
evidence.summary: Official joint meeting minutes list Anthea Brady as Select Board vice-chair following the 2025 election.

evidence.title: Spoke in support of amended school budget
evidence.summary: During Town Meeting coverage, Brady was quoted saying the schools obviously needed help with math, the McCall schedule needed to come into compliance with the rest of the district, and it was time for the town to make an investment now.

name: Christian Nixon
background: Architect with two degrees from Rice University and an education policy fellowship from Northeastern University’s Institute for Educational Leadership. He said he oversees budgeting, design, and construction of capital projects for a nearby community and school district, with more than 25 years of K-12 education and healthcare project experience.
summary: Incumbent School Committee member and longtime Town Meeting member who ran for another term in 2025 emphasizing school funding, sustainable town revenue, facilities planning, labor relations, and Lincoln and Muraco school capital needs. He lost in the two-seat race.
locationNote: Has lived in Winchester for 26 years, according to his candidate profile.
priorRecordSummary: Served on the School Committee from 2010 to the time of his 2025 profile, and on multiple town finance and capital planning bodies including Town Meeting, the EFPBC, Capital Planning Committee, and Audit Advisory Committee.

stanceCards.label: Argued for full FY26 funding case
stanceCards.detail: Listed making the case for Winchester Public Schools’ true FY26 funding needs as a top priority and pointed to use of the special education reserve fund to protect educational quality.

stanceCards.label: Supports sustainable revenue models
stanceCards.detail: Said he wants to support the State of the Town’s goals toward sustainable revenue models to meet long-term town and district needs.

stanceCards.label: Prioritized Muraco, Lincoln, and facilities planning
stanceCards.detail: Highlighted submitting the New Muraco statement of interest, completing Phase II of the 10-year facilities master plan, and securing funding for Lincoln Elementary improvements.

stanceCards.label: Emphasized reserve-fund governance and cross-board coordination
stanceCards.detail: Described the special education reserve fund as a budget management tool with Select Board concurrence as a check-and-balance, and stressed understanding the roles of town bodies for effective collaboration.

evidence.title: Candidate profile lists town service and professional background
evidence.summary: Nixon said he has served on Town Meeting since 2008 and the School Committee since 2010, along with the EFPBC, Capital Planning Committee, and Audit Advisory Committee. He also described his architecture and education policy background and capital-project budgeting experience.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines 2025 priorities
evidence.summary: He identified top priorities including FY26 school funding needs, sustainable revenue models, a New Muraco MSBA statement of interest, Phase II of the facilities master plan, WEA culture and communication concerns, a new collective bargaining agreement, and Lincoln Elementary improvements.

evidence.title: Explained use of special education reserve fund
evidence.summary: In discussing budget management, Nixon said the School Committee approved use of more than $360,000 from the special education reserve fund to cover unbudgeted special education costs and protect the FY25 budget, noting the fund was established by Town Meeting in 2018 and requires Select Board concurrence for transfers.

evidence.title: 2025 official election results
evidence.summary: Official results show Stefanie Mnayarji received 1,964 votes, Karen Bolognese 1,512, and Christian Nixon 975 in the School Committee race for two seats.

evidence.title: Election-night summary identifies School Committee winners
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that Mnayarji and incumbent Karen Maruyama Bolognese won the two School Committee seats, while incumbent Christian Nixon received 975 votes.

name: Dorothy R. Simboli
background: Lawyer with commercial and residential real estate experience; 25-year Winchester resident and mother of three adult children. Served on the Winchester Zoning Board of Appeals, including as chair, and as a Town Meeting member.
summary: Simboli ran for Select Board in 2025 emphasizing housing growth, business-friendly development, education quality, and tighter fiscal planning amid the town’s structural deficit. She said the town should consider a right-sized override as part of a longer-term plan and called for closer scrutiny of budget line items, including school spending.
locationNote: Winchester resident for 25 years; Town Meeting member from Precinct 6 and Zoning Board of Appeals chair.
priorRecordLabel: ZBA chair and Town Meeting member
priorRecordSummary: Served on the Zoning Board of Appeals from 2016 onward, becoming full member in 2023 and chair in 2024; also served as a Town Meeting member since 2016. At 2025 Town Meeting, she publicly asked how the school literacy pilot would measure success.
keyThemes: Balanced housing growth and historic preservation
Education quality including literacy, math, special education, and staff support
Structural deficit response through detailed budget review and long-term planning
Transparent budgeting and sound fiscal policy

stanceCards.label: Scrutinize school spending
stanceCards.detail: Said the school budget warrants a deep dive into line items, including special education costs, to evaluate how resources can be used optimally while maintaining essential services.

stanceCards.label: Supports literacy improvement with accountability
stanceCards.detail: Listed literacy as a critical school issue and, at Town Meeting, asked how the district would determine success for the literacy pilot program.

stanceCards.label: Focus on capital planning for school facilities
stanceCards.detail: Said the town faces deferred capital projects including the Lynch School and future Muraco project, requiring sound fiscal policies and prudent resource allocation.

stanceCards.label: Expand commercial tax base
stanceCards.detail: Called for a more business-friendly town, streamlined permitting, and parking solutions to support economic development and broaden revenue sources.

stanceCards.label: Open to a right-sized override
stanceCards.detail: At the candidate forum, said the town should consider an override, but that it must be strategically planned and right sized.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Simboli described her legal and real-estate background, service on the Zoning Board of Appeals and Town Meeting, and priorities including housing, education, fiscal stability, and economic development.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on structural deficit and budget review
evidence.summary: She said the town’s structural deficit requires creative evaluation of detailed budgets, noted rising health insurance and energy costs, suggested rebidding health insurance, and called for a deep dive into school budget line items including special education transportation.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on affordable housing and business development
evidence.summary: She supported more collaborative affordable housing projects, cited 160 Forest Street as an opportunity for affordable housing, and proposed streamlining permitting and studying parking options to support businesses.

evidence.title: Select Board forum on override and capital planning
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Simboli said the town should consider a right-sized override with strategic planning and said she wanted to get involved earlier in the budget process. She also suggested using EDA and CPA resources for capital planning.

evidence.title: Town Meeting question on literacy pilot accountability
evidence.summary: During Town Meeting debate on literacy funding, Simboli asked how the schools would determine success after the pilot program was over.

evidence.title: 2025 election result
evidence.summary: In the four-candidate Select Board race for two seats, Simboli received 806 votes and did not win a seat.

name: Jack LeMenager
background: Winchester resident since 1988; longtime Town Meeting member and member of the Town Meeting Members Association executive board. He has served on the Historical Commission since 2009, including the past eight years as chair, and has also served on town committees including the train station study committee and the Community Preservation Act Advisory Committee. He previously worked in management consulting and employee communications and said he had time as a recent retiree to devote to town service.
summary: LeMenager ran a write-in campaign for Planning Board focused on encouraging mixed-use and commercial development while preserving Winchester’s architectural character. He emphasized better coordination among town boards and a more predictable development process. He won the open Planning Board seat in 2025.
locationNote: Winchester resident; campaign centered on town development issues including North Main, the Center Business District, Converse Place, and the Waterfield Lot.
priorRecordLabel: Historical Commission and town committee experience
priorRecordSummary: LeMenager did not present prior Planning Board service in the provided sources, but cited long service on the Historical Commission, Town Meeting, and several town committees involving preservation and planning-related coordination.

stanceCards.label: Better coordination across boards
stanceCards.detail: He said boards and commissions should work together more closely and that the Planning Board should communicate more with the Select Board to anticipate opportunities rather than react later.

stanceCards.label: Encourage commercial development
stanceCards.detail: He said it is critical to encourage more commercial and residential development, with particular emphasis on adding more commercial space in areas like North Main.

stanceCards.label: Support for mixed-use housing growth
stanceCards.detail: He supported greater mixed-use development, including multi-story buildings with residences and retail, but framed it around right-size growth that fits Winchester’s built environment.

evidence.title: Write-in campaign focused on mixed-use development and coordination
evidence.summary: LeMenager said he was motivated to run to encourage more communication between boards, support right-size mixed-use development, and improve the approval process without sacrificing town values or architectural integrity.

evidence.title: Background in Historical Commission and Town Meeting
evidence.summary: The profile says he has served on the Historical Commission since 2009, the past eight years as chair, has been a Town Meeting member for 15 years, and has served on several ad hoc town committees.

evidence.title: Official 2025 Planning Board result
evidence.summary: Official results show Jack LeMenager received 739 write-in votes in the Planning Board race, ahead of Matthew Zarracina with 146 and other write-ins with 129.

evidence.title: Election-night report confirms win
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that LeMenager won the open Planning Board seat being vacated by Diab Jerius with 739 write-in votes.

name: Karen Maruyama Bolognese
background: Veterinarian and former finance executive with an MBA; has lived in Winchester 14 years. She has served on the School Committee since 2019 and was chair in 2021-22 and 2024-25, alongside other town roles including Town Meeting and facilities-related working groups.
summary: Incumbent School Committee member reelected in 2025. She emphasizes long-term budgeting, fiscal sustainability, broad student opportunity, and a process-driven approach to curriculum decisions, including elementary literacy review.
locationNote: Lives in Winchester; reported 14 years in town.
priorRecordLabel: Incumbent School Committee chair and member
priorRecordSummary: School Committee member since 2019, chair in 2021-22 and 2024-25; also served on Town Meeting and town working groups tied to facilities and housing planning.
keyThemes: Support for district resources and staffing
Broad opportunities across academics, arts, sports, and special education

stanceCards.label: Long-term budget planning
stanceCards.detail: Advocates a long-term, planful approach to budgeting through the Winchester 2030 strategic plan and budget process, with priorities across staffing, programming, professional development, and facilities.

stanceCards.label: Balances school needs with tax burden
stanceCards.detail: Says fiscal responsibility matters and explicitly recognizes the tax burden on residents while aiming to keep schools financially sustainable over the long term.

stanceCards.label: Supports superintendent’s literacy process
stanceCards.detail: Backs the superintendent’s approach in which educators and literacy specialists review, pilot, and recommend elementary literacy materials and supports before School Committee action.

stanceCards.label: Prefers controlled use over a full ban
stanceCards.detail: At the candidate forum, said phones are generally expected to stay in backpacks and argued education about wise usage and partnership with families are important; she said a total ban on devices would be difficult.

stanceCards.label: Links facilities to long-term planning
stanceCards.detail: Says she has worked to prioritize long-term district needs including school facilities, and has also served on the Facilities Master Plan Phase I Enrollment Update working group.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on background and priorities
evidence.summary: Describes her professional background, years in Winchester, School Committee service since 2019, other town roles, and priorities including broad student opportunity and fiscally sustainable budgeting.

evidence.title: Supports process-driven literacy review
evidence.summary: Says she fully supports the superintendent’s literacy approach centered on educators and literacy specialists reviewing materials, assessments, pilots, and recommendations before committee action.

evidence.title: Forum comments on technology policy
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, she said phones generally must stay in backpacks, questioned enforceability of a total ban, and emphasized education about wise usage and partnership with families.

evidence.title: 2025 election result
evidence.summary: Official results show Karen Bolognese won one of two School Committee seats with 1,512 votes.

evidence.title: Post-election committee chair listing
evidence.summary: May 30, 2025 State of the Town minutes list Karen Maruyama Bolognese as School Committee chair.

evidence.title: Local news summary of election winners
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that Stefanie Mnayarji and incumbent Karen Maruyama Bolognese won the two School Committee seats, with Bolognese receiving 1,512 votes.

name: Paras Bhayani
background: Corporate finance and business strategy professional with prior public-sector budget experience, including leading a $1.7 billion operating and capital budget for Chicago's transit authority. Winchester resident for five years with two young daughters.
summary: Bhayani ran on fiscal stewardship, combining efficiency measures with likely new revenue, while also emphasizing capital planning, infill housing, economic development, and climate/sustainability. He highlighted education advocacy through early literacy work and won one of two Select Board seats in 2025.
locationNote: Lives in Winchester and said he and his family moved to town for the schools and community.
priorRecordLabel: Education advocacy and outside public finance experience
priorRecordSummary: He did not cite prior elected or appointed Winchester town office, but described himself as a founding member of the Winchester Literacy Initiative for Change and cited major public-sector budget experience outside Winchester.
keyThemes: Fiscal stewardship and balanced town/school funding

stanceCards.label: Balanced funding with added revenue likely
stanceCards.detail: He said Winchester needs a combination of efficiencies and new revenue to balance the budget while protecting services, and said additional revenue would likely be needed for town and school operating and capital needs.

stanceCards.label: Early literacy involvement
stanceCards.detail: He cited his role as a founding member of the Winchester Literacy Initiative for Change, a parent-led group focused on strengthening early literacy curriculum in Winchester Public Schools.

stanceCards.label: Use technology for efficiency
stanceCards.detail: He proposed off-the-shelf technology to automate town processes and also supported digitizing permits, applications, and approvals to help businesses.

stanceCards.label: Capital planning focus
stanceCards.detail: He called for tackling a backlog of capital projects and, at the forum, referred to a five-year plan and possible override funding dedicated to capital projects.

stanceCards.label: Open to override revenue
stanceCards.detail: He said the 2019 override had already lasted longer than intended and that additional revenue would likely be needed; at the candidate forum he said taxpayers would likely need to be asked for additional money.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Bhayani described extensive private- and public-sector financial experience, including managing Chicago transit authority budgets, and listed key campaign priorities including balanced town and school funding, capital projects, housing, economic development, and sustainability.

evidence.title: Candidate profile backs efficiency measures and likely new revenue
evidence.summary: He said Winchester would need both efficiencies and new revenue, suggested technology and purchasing changes to save money, and said additional revenue would likely be needed because inflation had outpaced Proposition 2½ limits.

evidence.title: Candidate profile highlights literacy advocacy
evidence.summary: He said he was a founding member of the Winchester Literacy Initiative for Change, focused on strengthening early literacy curriculum in Winchester Public Schools.

evidence.title: Select Board forum comments on override and capital planning
evidence.summary: At the March 5 candidate forum, Bhayani said Winchester needed a comprehensive long-term plan, that efficiencies alone would not be enough, and that taxpayers would likely need to provide additional money. He also called for a five-year capital plan and referenced an override dedicated to capital projects.

evidence.title: Official 2025 election results
evidence.summary: Official results show Bhayani received 1,945 votes in the Select Board race, the highest total among four candidates for two seats.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage confirms win
evidence.summary: Local election coverage reported that Bhayani and incumbent Anthea Brady won the two Select Board seats, with Bhayani receiving 1,945 votes.

name: Rick Welch
background: Real estate developer and home builder with 35 years of experience, including permitting, developing, and building multiple 40B projects in Massachusetts. He said he has served in the past decade as president of Mesiti Development Corporation, developing, planning, and building hundreds of senior living units.
summary: Welch presented himself as a development-oriented candidate focused on affordable housing, senior housing, and commercial growth. He said Winchester should address its structural deficit without cutting school or sports programs. In the 2025 Select Board race, he did not win one of the two seats.
locationNote: Welch said he has lived in Winchester for 35 years and that his three children graduated from Winchester Public Schools.
priorRecordSummary: Welch cited decades of private-sector experience in real estate development, including affordable housing and senior living projects, and said he has worked closely with town officials and boards on community development.
keyThemes: Protecting school and sports programs while addressing the deficit
Expanding the commercial tax base through mixed-use development
Using development experience to assist with town real estate matters

stanceCards.label: Supports affordable and senior housing
stanceCards.detail: Welch said his affordable housing development experience could help Winchester meet housing needs and that he wants 160 Forest Street to create opportunities for local seniors struggling to remain in town.

stanceCards.label: Favors tax-base growth through development
stanceCards.detail: He called for ambitious downtown plans with lower-level commercial space and upper-floor retail/residential uses, and said 55-plus projects can bring in tax revenue.

stanceCards.label: Would avoid cuts to schools
stanceCards.detail: Welch said the town should look for ways to address the deficit without touching school or sports programs.

stanceCards.label: Concerned about the structural deficit
stanceCards.detail: He described the increased budget deficit as concerning and said the town needs to closely examine ways to make up the shortfall.

evidence.title: Welch describes development background
evidence.summary: He said he is a real estate developer and home builder with 35 years of experience, including multiple 40B projects and senior living development as president of Mesiti Development Corporation.

evidence.title: Welch on budget deficit and schools
evidence.summary: He said the town should find ways to make up for the deficit without touching school or sports programs and said school spending appears well used.

evidence.title: Welch on 160 Forest Street and senior housing
evidence.summary: He said his affordable housing experience would help navigate Winchester's housing needs and that he would like 160 Forest Street to offer opportunities to Winchester seniors.

evidence.title: Welch on downtown growth and tax base
evidence.summary: He proposed mixed-use downtown development, reduced parking requirements for some units, and incentives for developers to build 55-plus projects to grow the tax base.

evidence.title: 2025 Select Board election result
evidence.summary: In the race for two Select Board seats, Richard Welch, Jr. received 674 votes, trailing Paras Bhayani (1945), Anthea Brady (1649), and Dorothy Simboli (806), and did not win a seat.

name: Stefanie Mnayarji
background: Economist with education at the London School of Economics and Northeastern University; worked in FinTech consulting for large financial institutions and later founded Luxxie Boston. Has lived in Winchester for eight years.
summary: Elected to the School Committee in 2025 after campaigning on school funding, literacy reform, broader curriculum review, intentional technology use, and school culture. Previously served as a Precinct 5 Town Meeting Member and in several school and parent leadership roles.
locationNote: Lives in Winchester and said she had lived there for eight years as of February 2025.
priorRecordLabel: Town Meeting and school parent leadership
priorRecordSummary: Reported prior service as a Precinct 5 Town Meeting Member, co-chair of the Parents' Inter-School Council, TMMA Board member, Ambrose School Council member, and Ambrose PTO president. By May 2025, official town minutes list her as a School Committee member and Recording Secretary.

stanceCards.label: Backs literacy reform
stanceCards.detail: Identified literacy reform as a central reason for running and said curriculum updates should include classroom investment, coaching, professional development, and feedback loops.

stanceCards.label: Supports stricter device limits
stanceCards.detail: Called for intentional use of technology, including review of personal device policies, and at the candidate forum said she supports a phone-free, more broadly device-free, school experience.

stanceCards.label: Protect classroom spending
stanceCards.detail: Said budget management should prioritize classroom size, student-teacher ratios, direct student services, literacy and math curriculum, teacher support, and special education, while looking for administrative efficiencies and grants.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes transparent budgeting
stanceCards.detail: Called for transparent, collaborative budget decision-making with town boards, teachers, parents, and community members.

stanceCards.label: Links device limits to student well-being
stanceCards.detail: At the forum, cited reports from phone-free districts of better focus and improved mental health.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: background and motivations
evidence.summary: Mnayarji described her professional background, prior town and volunteer roles, and said she was running to address funding, curriculum, literacy reform, technology use, and school culture.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: budget priorities
evidence.summary: She said school budgeting should be fiscally responsible, data-driven, collaborative, and should protect classroom investments, special education, and literacy and math curricula while seeking grants and efficiencies.

evidence.title: Candidate forum: device-free schools position
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Mnayarji said she supports a phone-free and more broadly device-free school experience and argued such policies can improve focus and mental health.

evidence.title: 2025 official election results
evidence.summary: Official town results show Mnayarji received 1,964 votes in the School Committee race, the highest total in a two-seat contest.

evidence.title: Post-election committee service
evidence.summary: Official May 30, 2025 minutes list Stefanie Mnayarji as a Winchester School Committee member and Recording Secretary.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage confirms winners
evidence.summary: Local election coverage reported that Mnayarji and Karen Maruyama Bolognese won the two School Committee seats, while incumbent Christian Nixon lost.
